metric_value: Traffic reversed from 80% local/20% cross-subnet to 20% local/80% cross-subnet
notes: Aberdeen research reveals traffic pattern reversal driven by distributed client-server, IP protocols, Web-based technologies.

metric_value: Routers have become major performance bottleneck in enterprise networks with 80% cross-subnet traffic
notes: Increased transactions and network complexity have routers working overtime; traditional segmentation doesn't scale.

metric_value: March 3, 1997
notes: First member of the Switch Node product family; compact Layer 3 switching solution.

metric_name: Switch Node architecture - data/control plane separation
metric_value: Data plane (forwarding) and control plane (topology) run on separate 1.2 Gbps buses
notes: Separation enables both planes to be independently optimized for performance; foundational SDN-like architecture.

metric_value: Transparently integrates with existing network routers with no reconfiguration; automatically learns Layer 2 and Layer 3 addresses
notes: Dedicated high-speed CPU for learning; key feature enabling non-disruptive deployment into existing networks.

metric_value: Micro-second latency with wire speed switching performance
notes: Aberdeen states Switch Node brings 'micro-second latency and wire speed switching performance to routing.'

metric_value: Easy integration into existing networks with minimal configuration requirements
notes: Aberdeen interviews identify this as critical evaluation criterion; Switch Node addresses via IP AutoLearn.

metric_value: Capability to deliver scalable performance from subnet to subnet
notes: Scalable data forwarding via distributed processors addresses cross-subnet performance requirement.

metric_value: Ability to accommodate emerging real-time applications such as video
notes: Video networking already an emerging requirement in 1997 enterprise networking.

metric_value: Cost-effectiveness vs. router upgrades or ATM migration
notes: Aberdeen positions Switch Node as lower-cost alternative to expensive router upgrades or full ATM migration.

metric_value: 5-slot chassis; 16-port 10Base-T; 8-port 10Base-FL; 2-port 100Base-TX switching modules; RISC processors for wire-speed forwarding
notes: RISC processors enable fast address lookup on forwarding table caches; supports both 10Mbps and 100Mbps connections.

metric_value: IP Switching, Tag Switching, Flow Switching - migration strategies for high-speed network media
notes: Aberdeen contends Cisco and others' solutions are generally too complex for campus environments and not always relevant.

metric_value: Bay's approach 'right on target'; non-disruptive here-and-now alternative fitting existing network architecture
notes: Aberdeen explicitly endorses Bay's architectural approach over competing Layer 3/Tag/IP switching solutions.

metric_value: Bay Networks has seized window of opportunity to bring cost-effective Layer 3 switching solution to campus networks
notes: Aberdeen predicts Bay's Switch Node will succeed in addressing campus network performance needs.

metric_value: Bay Networks acquired by Nortel for $9.1B in June 1998; Switch Node product line discontinued post-acquisition; Cisco's Catalyst series dominated campus Layer 3 switching market through 2000s
notes: Source: Wired June 1998 Nortel acquisition; Bay Networks Wikipedia. Bay's campus switching products did not achieve market leadership before acquisition.

metric_value: ATM migration generally too complex for campus network environments and not always relevant
notes: Aberdeen dismisses ATM for campus use in favor of Bay's Layer 3 switching approach.

metric_value: ATM failed to achieve campus dominance; IP-based switching (Gigabit Ethernet) became campus backbone standard by 2000
notes: Aberdeen's prediction that ATM was too complex for campus use proved accurate. Gigabit Ethernet won the campus backbone battle.

metric_value: Switch Node based on same BayRS distributed multitasking routing software used in Bay Network routers; ensures routing consistency
notes: Software continuity from Bay's router line to Switch Node reduces operational complexity for existing Bay customers.

metric_value: Combining IP/IPX routing control with full wire speed performance; data plane forwarding separated from control plane topology
notes: The data/control plane separation concept Aberdeen highlights in 1997 anticipates the SDN architectural paradigm formalized circa 2008-2012.

metric_value: Non-disruptive integration strategy: fits existing network architecture, no reconfiguration of routers or clients required
notes: Contrasts with Cisco's more disruptive migration-based approach to high-speed networking.
